title: Kurzfassung: Nationale Berichterstattung „Pflanzenschutzmittelrückstände in Lebensmitteln 2024“
title_en: Germany 2024: National Report on Pesticide Residues in Food
summary_en: German Federal Office of Consumer Protection and Food Safety (BVL) publishes 2024 national monitoring report summarizing pesticide residue data in food products. The report is directly relevant to supply chain compliance and regulatory requirements for coffee and tea products distributed in Germany and the EU market.

title: Odluka o izravnim mjerama kontrole cijena određenih proizvoda i određenih kategorija proizvoda u trgovini na malo i Odluka o objavi cjenika i isticanju dodatne cijene kao mjera izravne kontrole cijene u trgovini na malo Izvješće o obavljenim inspekcijskim nadzorima od 7. veljače 2025. do 26. lipnja 2026. godine 01.07.2026.
title_en: Croatia Enforces Retail Price Controls; 429 Products Sold Above Maximum Permitted Prices
summary_en: Croatia's State Inspectorate documented enforcement of price control measures from February 2025 to June 2026, recording 429 product instances sold above legally permitted maximum prices and 2,346 products missing required visual price identification marks. The report identifies widespread compliance violations including failure to display price-restricted items in designated retail sections and inadequate price labeling. These findings demonstrate active regulatory monitoring and enforcement of retail pricing rules across the Croatian market.

title: 14.07.2026 Радомир Чолаков: С 400% са се повишили сигналите в КЗК за последната година Доверието в Комисията за защита на конкуренцията се повишава. Виждаме го по броя на сигналите, които получаваме – има ръст с 400% за последната година.
title_en: Bulgarian Competition Authority Reports 400% Surge in Complaints and Investigates Unfair Retailer Practices Against Food Producers
summary_en: Bulgaria's Competition Commission reported a 400% annual increase in complaints and initiated four cartel investigations in 2026 covering hospital food, construction equipment rental, and institutional food supply. The authority also began enforcement actions against two major retail chains for alleged unfair trading practices, including excessive discount demands on domestic food producers that restrict their market access and distribution. The CPC proposed 19 measures to strengthen competition in the food sector, with recent fines totaling €359,000 for misleading labeling and unfair commercial practices.
source_name: Bulgaria - Commission for Protection of Competition
entities: Bulgaria Commission for Protection of Competition

title: Междинен секторен анализ за представяне на предварителните заключения за състоянието на конкуренцията в сектора на модерната търговия на дребно със селскостопански и бързооборотни хранителни продукти
title_en: Bulgaria: Interim sectoral analysis on competition in modern retail trade for agricultural and FMCG products
summary_en: Bulgarian Competition Commission preliminary sectoral analysis examining competition conditions in modern retail trade of agricultural and fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG). JDE Peet's coffee and tea products fall within the FMCG retail category covered by this analysis, which may inform competitive positioning, shelf space allocation, and pricing dynamics in Bulgarian retail channels.
source_name: Bulgaria - Commission for Protection of Competition
entities: Bulgarian Commission for Protection of Competition

title: Cialde ESE 44 mm Caffè Borbone Miscela ORO
title_en: Caffè Borbone ESE 44mm Coffee Pods – Oro Blend
summary_en: Caffè Borbone (Italian independent producer) manufactures ESE 44mm compatible coffee pods compatible with Nespresso, Lavazza, Bialetti, and Dolce Gusto systems. Third-party compatible capsule producers represent significant competition in the aftermarket capsule segment where JDE Peet's operates.

title: illycaffè presents Arabica Selection Brasile Cerrado Mineiro in compatible capsules
title_en: illycaffè presents Arabica Selection Brasile Cerrado Mineiro in compatible capsules
summary_en: illycaffè has launched Arabica Selection Brasile Cerrado Mineiro coffee available in compatible capsule formats. This product launch in the capsule coffee segment is directly relevant to JDE Peet's market position in the Senseo and Tassimo capsule categories.

title: Q2 2026 Keurig Dr Pepper Earnings Conference Call
title_en: Keurig Dr Pepper announces Q2 2026 earnings conference call
summary_en: KDP announced Q2 2026 earnings conference call for Thursday, 6 August 2026 at 8:00 AM (ET), hosted by CEO Tim Cofer and CFO Anthony DiSilvestro to discuss results for quarter ended 30 June 2026. Consolidated earnings include JDE Peet's financial performance. Webcast and dial-in access provided with replay available for two weeks.

title: Keurig Dr Pepper to Report Second Quarter 2026 Results and Host Conference Call
title_en: Keurig Dr Pepper to report Q2 2026 financial results on 6 August 2026
summary_en: KDP will release Q2 2026 (ended 30 June 2026) financial results before market open on Thursday, 6 August 2026, followed by conference call at 8:00 AM (ET) with CEO Tim Cofer and CFO Anthony DiSilvestro. Consolidated results include JDE Peet's coffee business performance across 100+ markets. Replay available through 20 August 2026.

title: KEURIG DR PEPPER HIGHLIGHTS PERFORMANCE AND PURPOSE-DRIVEN OUTCOMES IN 2025 IMPACT REPORT Read More
title_en: Keurig Dr Pepper releases 2025 Impact Report: climate action, circular economy progress, supply chain resilience
summary_en: KDP released its 2025 Impact Report covering seven impact areas: climate and nature action with investment in regenerative agriculture; circular economy with 96% of packaging recyclable/compostable and expanded K-Cup recycling; strengthened responsible coffee and cocoa sourcing; consumer health with 10% added sugar reduction in U.S. Refreshment Beverages since 2020; employee volunteerism nearly doubling; and corporate governance commitments. Commitments apply across KDP operations including JDE Peet's.

title: Human Rights, Responsible Sourcing & Supply Chain Livelihoods
title_en: Keurig Dr Pepper's human rights and responsible coffee sourcing commitment
summary_en: KDP outlined its commitment to responsible sourcing and human rights due diligence across supply chains, particularly coffee and cocoa. The company emphasizes multi-stakeholder partnerships (suppliers, producer organizations, NGOs) to uphold human rights standards, ensure farmer economic resilience, and prevent forced and child labor. KDP has established policies including Corporate Code of Conduct, Supplier Code of Conduct, Brazilian Coffee Purchasing Policy, Conflict Minerals Policy, and publishes grievance mechanisms and due diligence reporting.

title: Employee Health, Safety & Well-Being
title_en: Keurig Dr Pepper announces employee health, safety and well-being strategy
summary_en: KDP outlined group-wide employee strategy emphasizing inclusive culture, career development, total rewards, workplace safety, wellness programs, and community engagement. Strategy includes hybrid work flexibility, mental and physical health resources, learning and development platforms, and employee resource groups. This applies across all operating units including JDE Peet's workforce.

title: Press Releases 27 Mar 2026 JDE Peet’s transfers shares and grants awards to participants under its employee incentive plans
title_en: JDE Peet's transfers shares and grants awards to participants under employee incentive plans
summary_en: JDE Peet's announced on 27 March 2026 the grant of 811,205 conditional rights (RSUs and PSUs) to 204 participants and transfer of 12,955 shares to 14 participants under employee incentive plans. This disclosure is required under Dutch offer rules in connection with Keurig Dr Pepper's public takeover offer. Total issued share capital remains unchanged at 488,178,642 shares.

title: Press Releases 30 Jul 2025 JDE Peet’s reports half-year results 2025
title_en: JDE Peet's H1 2025 results: strong sales growth and strategic portfolio realignment
summary_en: JDE Peet's reported H1 2025 organic sales growth of 22.5% (21.5% price, 1.0% volume/mix) with organic adjusted EBIT growth of 2.0% amid persistent high green coffee prices. New product launches included Peet's Popping Pearls, L'OR Coconut Iced Espresso, Jacobs Dubai Chocolate, and Moccona Liquid Espresso. Strategic actions included divestment of Turkish tea business, discontinuation of L'OR Barista U.S. roll-out, transfer of L'OR capsules business to Peet's, announced closure of Banbury U.K. factory, and launch of "Reignite the Amazing" strategy on 1 July 2025.

title: The hidden flaw in global supply chains: why optimisation alone is no longer enough
title_en: Global Supply Chain Resilience Beyond Optimization Models
summary_en: The article examines how traditional supply chain optimization models are no longer sufficient in managing persistent global disruptions. Modern supply chains require resilience strategies beyond mathematical cost reduction, as companies face continuous challenges rather than occasional disruptions.

title: The Loadstar Explainer Podcast: El Niño and its impact on shipping
title_en: El Niño and Extreme Weather Impacts on Global Shipping Routes
summary_en: Extreme weather events including El Niño patterns and unusual Atlantic conditions are affecting global shipping operations. Maritime operators are transitioning from predictive models to real-time decision-making strategies.

title: Equipment fatigue is eroding brand loyalty in specialty coffee
title_en: Consumer decision fatigue in specialty coffee equipment erodes brand loyalty
summary_en: The specialty coffee equipment market is experiencing rapid product proliferation that creates consumer fatigue rather than engagement, fragmenting brand loyalty. With 87% of US consumers preparing coffee at home and manufacturers accelerating product releases, consumers increasingly view equipment as temporary rather than long-term investments, altering purchase patterns and brand retention dynamics.

title: Major brands are divesting from physical café locations and investing in coffee distribution networks
title_en: Major brands shift from physical retail to scalable distribution networks
summary_en: Large coffee industry players are divesting from physical café operations and pivoting to consumer packaged goods distribution models due to rising overhead costs and operational complexity. Nestlé's US$7.15 billion acquisition of Starbucks' global CPG distribution rights exemplifies this strategic shift, indicating that scalable distribution is becoming the primary engine of growth at the expense of hospitality-based business models.

title: Regenerative agriculture is about more than just sustainability
title_en: Regenerative agriculture is about more than just sustainability
summary_en: Major coffee multinationals including Nespresso, Starbucks, Illy, and Lavazza are actively investing in regenerative agriculture initiatives and pursuing Regenerative Organic Certified (ROC) status; nearly 150 ROC-certified brands now exist globally. Beyond environmental sustainability, regenerative agriculture serves as a business asset and differentiation tool, offering a concrete value proposition that cuts through abstract sustainability standards and appeals to values-conscious consumers.

title: Why luxury fashion brands are opening specialty coffee shops in China
title_en: Why luxury fashion brands are opening specialty coffee shops in China
summary_en: Luxury fashion brands (Ralph Lauren, Dior, Burberry, Louis Vuitton, Armani, Chanel, Fendi, Bulgari) are opening specialty coffee shops within flagship stores in China to attract Gen Z and millennial consumers, who represent 66% of the country's coffee consumption. The Chinese coffee market nearly doubled from US$7 billion (2015) to US$12 billion (2020), driven by younger demographics seeking premium experiences; coffee serves as a lifestyle differentiation and foot-traffic driver for luxury retail.

title: Why innovations for sustainable coffee agriculture aren't being adopted
title_en: Why innovations for sustainable coffee agriculture aren't being adopted
summary_en: Sustainable agricultural innovations for coffee (precision harvesting equipment, programmable irrigation, soil-health monitoring) show significant potential but face sluggish adoption rates; 47% of industry respondents cite lack of funding as the primary barrier. A key impediment is the gap between academic researchers and producers in the field: researchers develop untailored solutions that do not address farmers' practical constraints, investment capacity, or immediate operational needs.

title: The coffee industry's obsession with Gen Z may be misplaced
title_en: Coffee industry's Gen Z obsession may misread future consumption patterns
summary_en: Coffee Intelligence analysis argues beverage industry overweights Gen Z trend prediction despite US$12 trillion in Gen Z-Millennial spending growth by 2030. Social media amplification of viral drinks and temporary fads may obscure broader demographic and cultural forces shaping long-term coffee consumption.

title: Craft beer's decline offers a warning for specialty coffee
title_en: Craft beer's decline offers a warning for specialty coffee
summary_en: The US craft beer industry, which grew from fewer than 2,000 breweries in 2010 to over 9,500 by the early 2020s, is now experiencing closures outpacing openings for the first time since 2005, signalling a shift from rapid expansion to consolidation. Specialty coffee is exhibiting similar trajectories—market saturation, rising closures among specialty roasters, and consumer fatigue with constant novelty—suggesting the industry may be entering a mature, consolidating phase where consistency and strong brand identity matter more than experimental offerings.

title: The real “coffee hunters” are at origin – not in consuming countries
title_en: The real "coffee hunters" are at origin – not in consuming countries
summary_en: Over 90% of coffee exported from producing countries is shipped as green coffee, and approximately 90% of the work identifying outstanding coffee lots occurs at origin through pre-selection, evaluation, and processing by exporters, producers, and local quality teams—often long before buyers arrive for sourcing trips. This challenges the narrative that specialty coffee 'discovery' happens through buyer visits, highlighting the critical expertise and infrastructure required at origin to match coffees to buyer requirements.

title: Why private label isn't the best idea for roasters
title_en: Why private label isn't the best idea for roasters
summary_en: The global private label market grew from US$271 billion in 2024 to nearly US$330 billion in 2025, with some specialty roasters increasingly offering white-label or secondary brands. While private label roasting can address short-term profitability by filling spare capacity, it risks converting roasters from brand builders into manufacturers, potentially eroding long-term brand equity and strategic positioning.

title: Alternative products: what do they mean for coffee?
title_en: Alternative products: what do they mean for coffee?
summary_en: Lab-grown and plant-based alternatives to coffee are emerging amid climate volatility and supply-chain uncertainty, with approximately 65% of Gen Z consumers in the US actively seeking plant-based options. However, most alternatives remain a small fraction of food consumption; the key question is whether they will scale to displace traditional coffee, particularly as consumers face financial pressures and prior initiatives (e.g., Beyond Meat) have experienced sales slumps.

title: Specialty coffee now cares more about consistency than craft
title_en: Specialty coffee now cares more about consistency than craft
summary_en: The specialty coffee sector is undergoing a fundamental shift away from artisanal craft toward automation, consistency, and standardisation to compete in scaled global markets. Major investments in automated roasting systems (IMF, Loring) and precision brewing equipment, coupled with Starbucks' US$450M automation investment, demonstrate that consistency and repeatability—not hand-crafted novelty—are now the industry's competitive focus, mirroring the industrialisation specialty coffee once opposed.

title: Transport und Lagerung des Rohkaffees
title_en: Coffee supply chain: trading infrastructure, commodity pricing, and market structure
summary_en: German Coffee Association provides an overview of coffee supply chain infrastructure from production to consumption. The analysis details international trading actors (importers, exporters, roasters, and commodity exchanges in New York and London), documents that 95% of coffee trades as raw commodity before processing, and identifies key price formation factors including supply/demand dynamics, inventory levels, harvest cycles, commodity market speculation, and seasonal consumption patterns.

title: Energiemonitor ACM: variabele consumententarieven voor gas deze maand met 7% omhoog
title_en: Energy Monitor July 2026: Dutch gas tariffs rise 7%, electricity 3%
summary_en: Variable consumer gas tariffs in the Netherlands increased 7% in July 2026, now 12% above pre-Iran-war levels, while electricity tariffs rose only 3% due to renewable energy expansion. Dutch renewable generation (wind and solar) now represents approximately half of all electricity production; the country exported 7.7 TWh in H1 2026, primarily to Belgium, Germany, and the UK.
source_name: Netherlands - Authority for Consumers and Markets (ACM)

L'enquÃªte de la Commission se concentre sur des fonctionnalitÃ©s telles que le dÃ©filement infini,...
title_en: European Commission concludes Meta's Instagram and Facebook design violates Digital Services Act
summary_en: The European Commission concluded preliminarily on 10 July 2026 that the addictive design of Instagram and Facebook violates the Digital Services Act. The Commission found inadequate risk assessment regarding features such as infinite scroll, autoplay, push notifications, and highly personalized recommendations that fuel compulsive use and excessive screen time, particularly harming minors and vulnerable adults. Meta's risk mitigation measures, including time-management tools and parental controls, were assessed as insufficient and easily circumvented.

title: Už pranešimus apie kartelius – iki 100 tūkst. eurų išmoka 2026 05 04
title_en: Lithuania launches cartel whistleblower reward program offering up to €100,000
summary_en: The Lithuanian Competition Council announced a whistleblower reward program offering financial incentives of up to €100,000 for reporting cartels and anti-competitive agreements. Competition Council Chair Jolanta Ivanauskienė emphasized that cartels typically raise prices by 10–20% and cause significant economic damage. The program aims to encourage disclosure of secret price-fixing and bid-rigging schemes affecting consumers and public procurement.

title: Konkurenciją ribojantys susitarimai
title_en: Anti-Competitive Agreements Enforcement and Whistleblower Rewards in Lithuania
summary_en: Lithuania's Competition Council prohibits agreements restricting competition and offers monetary rewards (EUR 1,000–100,000) to individuals providing evidence of cartel activity. Reward amounts depend on evidentiary significance, infringement severity and duration, consumer impact, and cooperation level with authorities.

title: Piktnaudžiavimas dominuojančia padėtimi
title_en: Abuse of Dominant Market Position Prohibition in Lithuania
summary_en: Lithuania prohibits abuse of dominant market position under competition law and TFEU Article 102, imposing stricter obligations on dominant firms than competitors. Prohibited conduct includes imposing unfair prices or terms, restricting trade or technical progress, applying discriminatory conditions, and imposing unjustified contractual obligations. Penalties reach up to 10% of global annual turnover, with individual liability for company officers (bans from management 3–5 years, fines up to EUR 14,481).

title: Atļaujas saņemšana veselīguma norādes lietošanai
title_en: Health Claims Authorization Procedures in Latvia (EU Regulation 1924/2006)
summary_en: Companies making health claims on food products in Latvia must comply with EU Regulation 1924/2006 and obtain authorization through EFSA evaluation. Functional claims and disease-risk reduction claims require scientific substantiation and are evaluated either through EU-wide registration or individual company applications. Health claims for children and those based on proprietary data require enhanced scientific evidence and approval procedures.

title: Izmaiņu izdarīšana noteiktās grupās ietilpstošiem pārtikas produktiem
title_en: Food Product Labeling Change Notification Requirements in Latvia
summary_en: Food businesses in Latvia must immediately notify the Food and Veterinary Service of any labeling changes for food products in specified categories. Notifications must include a formal statement, new label text samples in Latvian, and original label samples or clear photocopies. The FVD processes notifications and updates the product registry within one month of receipt.

title: Specifiskās marķēšanas prasības ĢMO vai ĢMO saturošiem produktiem
title_en: GMO Labeling Requirements for Food Products
summary_en: EU Regulations 1829/2003 and 1830/2003 establish mandatory labeling for genetically modified organisms and products containing or derived from GMO. Products with GMO ingredients above 0.9% per component must declare this in the ingredients list using phrases like 'genetically modified' or 'produced from genetically modified [ingredient name]'. Supply chain operators must maintain traceability documentation and demonstrate compliance.

title: Piesārņotāju maksimāli pieļaujamie līmeņi
title_en: Maximum Residue Limits and Food Safety Contaminant Standards
summary_en: EU regulations establish enforceable maximum limits for pesticide residues (Regulation 396/2005), microbiological contaminants (Regulation 2073/2005), and chemical contaminants (Regulation 1881/2006) in all food products. Coffee and tea must comply with these maximum residue levels (MRL) to be marketed in the EU; exceedances trigger product rejection, recalls, and enforcement actions. Compliance verification is mandatory at import and throughout the supply chain.

title: Materiālu un izstrādājumu, kas paredzēti saskarei ar pārtiku, robežkontrole
title_en: Food Contact Materials Compliance: Plastics, Packaging, and Article Standards
summary_en: EU regulations require declarations of conformity for all food-contact materials including plastics (Regulation 10/2011), ceramics, rubber, and regenerated cellulose. Recycled plastic materials must comply with Regulation 2022/1616 and be registered in EU databases. Non-EU manufacturers of food-contact articles must meet equivalent EU standards. Compliance documentation and technical files are mandatory for market entry.

title: Specifiskās marķēšanas prasības augu izcelsmes produktiem (t.s. cukuram, sālim, uztura bagātinātājiem, utt.)
title_en: Specific Labeling Requirements for Plant-Origin Food Products
summary_en: EU and Latvian regulations for labeling plant-based food products, including dietary foods, fortified products, cocoa/chocolate items, and other plant-origin categories. Establishes classification, quality, nutritional disclosure, and marking standards. Applies to coffee and tea as plant-derived beverage products sold in EU/EEA.

title: Specifiskās marķēšanas prasības bezalkoholiskiem un alkoholiskiem dzērieniem
title_en: Specific Labeling Requirements for Non-Alcoholic and Alcoholic Beverages
summary_en: EU and Latvian regulatory framework for beverage labeling and classification. References EU Regulation 1308/2013 establishing common agricultural product market organization. Sets quality, safety, classification, and specific labeling requirements for beverage products. Directly applicable to coffee and tea as non-alcoholic beverages sold in EU/EEA markets.

title: Specifiskās marķēšanas prasības bioloģiskās lauksaimniecības produktiem
title_en: Specific Labeling Requirements for Organic Agriculture Products
summary_en: EU regulatory framework for organic product certification and labeling. References EU Regulations 834/2007, 889/2008, and 1235/2008 establishing organic production standards, certification requirements, and import procedures for third-country organic products. Applicable to JDE Peet's if marketing organic coffee, tea, or other organic beverage variants in EU/EEA.

title: Pārtikas nekaitīguma robežkontrolei pakļautās preces
title_en: Food Products Subject to Food Safety Border Control
summary_en: Latvia Food and Veterinary Service regulatory overview of food and beverage products subject to border safety controls. Establishes standards for contaminant limits, food additives usage, general and specific labeling requirements including for plant-origin products, organic products, GMO products, and irradiated foods. Applies to all food/beverage imports and sales in Latvia and EU.

title: Prasības atbilstībai piesārņotāju maksimāli pieļaujamiem līmeņiem (mikrobioloģija,pesticīdi, toksīni, smagie metāli uc.)
title_en: Maximum Permissible Contaminant Levels in Food (Microbiology, Pesticides, Toxins, Heavy Metals)
summary_en: EU regulatory framework establishing maximum permissible levels of microbial, pesticide, toxin, and heavy metal contamination in food and beverage products. References Commission Regulation 2073/2005 (microbiological criteria), EU Regulation 2023/915 (contaminant limits), and Regulation 396/2005 (pesticide residues). Mandatory compliance for all food/beverage products sold in EU/EEA.

title: Pārtikas uzlabotāju (piedevu, aromatizētāju, enzīmu) lietošanas un kontroli reglamentējošie normatīvie akti
title_en: Food Additives, Flavorings, and Enzymes: Regulatory Requirements
summary_en: EU regulatory framework governing food additives, flavorings, and enzymes permitted in food and beverage production. References EU Regulations 1333/2008 (additives), 1332/2008 (enzymes), and 1334/2008 (flavorings). Specifies permitted substances, usage limits, labeling requirements, and quality standards. Directly applicable to coffee and tea product formulations.

title: Normatīvie akti Informācija par normatīvajiem aktiem, ar kuriem saskaņā darbojas Konkurences padome.
title_en: Normative Acts Governing Competition and Fair Trading in Latvia
summary_en:
Reference compendium of regulatory framework under which Latvia's Competition Council operates: Competition Law (antitrust, merger control, abuse of dominance), Unfair Retail Trade Practices Prohibition Law (NMPAL), Unfair Trading Practices Law, and Marketing Law. Includes references to international competition law standards and OECD/EU guidelines.

For food and beverage suppliers operating in Latvia, this framework establishes legal obligations for fair trading practices, merger notifications, compliance with buyer power regulations, and exposure to competition enforcement by the Council.

title: Vēsture Konkurences padomes vēsturiskā attīstība no 1991.gada.
title_en: History of Latvia Competition Council: Expansion of Unfair Trading Practices Regulation (2021-2022)
summary_en:
Latvia's Competition Council history documents two major regulatory developments affecting food and beverage distribution: (1) on 1 November 2021, the comprehensive Unfair Trading Practices Law entered into force, prohibiting unfair commercial practices throughout the entire agricultural and food products supply chain (and prohibiting buyer power abuse in non-food retail), significantly expanding the prior retail-focused NMPAL; (2) on 23 June 2022, amendments to the Competition Law strengthened the Council's independence, institutional protections, resources, and enforcement powers for effective investigation and prevention of competition violations.

These reforms indicate Latvia is actively strengthening supplier protection across the food and beverage value chain and aligning with broader EU enforcement trends.

title: Konference godīgai mazumtirgotāju un piegādātāju sadarbībai
title_en: Conference: Fair Retailer-Supplier Cooperation
summary_en:
Latvia's Competition Council held a 2015 conference presenting official implementation guidelines for the Unfair Retail Trade Practices Prohibition Law (NMPAL), which entered into force on 1 January 2016. The law aims to balance bargaining power between large retailers and their food/beverage suppliers by prohibiting unfair commercial practices in procurement relationships.

The guidelines established practical compliance framework defining prohibited practices (e.g., unilateral contract changes, unreasonable payment terms, arbitrary demands) and provided market participants with standards for fair buyer-supplier cooperation in food supply chains.

title: Konference “Tirgotāju un piegādātāju sadarbība: Vai likums nodrošina līdzsvaru?”
title_en: Conference: Retailer-Supplier Cooperation - Does the Law Provide Balance?
summary_en:
Latvia's Competition Council and Chamber of Commerce held a 2017 conference reviewing the first year of implementation of the Unfair Retail Trade Practices Prohibition Law (NMPAL, effective 1 January 2016). Participants from major retailers, traders' associations, and government discussed practical gains and challenges in applying the law, which aims to balance buyer power in procurement relationships between large retailers and suppliers.

The conference examined how NMPAL has changed retailer-supplier cooperation dynamics and whether the legal framework adequately protects suppliers from abusive buyer practices in negotiations and contract terms.
entities: Latvia Chamber of Commerce and Industry

title: Negodīgas mazumtirdzniecības prakses aizlieguma likums
title_en: Unfair Retail Trading Practices Prohibition Law
summary_en: Latvia enforces a law prohibiting unfair retail trading practices and sets penalties for violations of competition law and retail conduct rules. Directly governs JDE Peet's retail relationships, pricing, promotional practices, and supply agreements with retailers and distributors in Latvia.

title: Grozījumi Atkritumu apsaimniekošanas likumā
title_en: Amendments to Waste Management Law – Batteries and E-Waste
summary_en: Latvia amended its Waste Management Law to implement EU Regulation 2023/1542 on batteries and establish a deposit system for single-use electronic smoking devices. Changes affect classification and disposal of batteries and electronic waste, impacting packaging compliance and waste management obligations for beverage companies.

title: Contributo agli oneri di funzionamento dell'AGCM
title_en: AGCM Reduces Operating Contribution Fee to 0.055 Per Thousand of Revenue
summary_en: Italy's AGCM (antitrust authority) reduced the mandatory operating contribution for businesses via deliberation n. 31871 dated 3 March 2026. The new rate is 0.055 per thousand of audited annual revenue, with payment required by 31 July each year. This replaces a higher fee structure and applies to all enterprises operating in Italy under AGCM supervision.
entities: AGCM - Autorità Garante della Concorrenza e del Mercato

title: CCPC opening statement to Joint Committee on Finance, Public Expenditure, Public Service Reform and Digitalisation, and Taoiseach
title_en: CCPC Expands Regulatory Mandate: New Enforcement Authority Under EU Data, AI, Accessibility, and Digital Services Acts
summary_en: The CCPC's enforcement mandate has been expanded to include the EU Data Act (data sharing and interoperability), AI Act (algorithmic governance and transparency), Digital Services Act (online platform accountability), and EU Accessibility Act (digital accessibility standards). These new responsibilities represent substantial compliance areas for businesses operating in the EU, with the CCPC implementing administrative enforcement powers beyond traditional competition and consumer protection enforcement.

title: CCPC welcomes increase in merger thresholds to reduce regulatory burden
title_en: Ireland Raises Merger Notification Thresholds Effective 1 July 2026
summary_en: Ireland's CCPC announced new mandatory merger notification thresholds effective 1 July 2026, raising the aggregate turnover threshold from €60 million to €100 million and individual business threshold from €10 million to €15 million. The change reduces regulatory burden on smaller deals while preserving the CCPC's call-in powers for deals below the new thresholds that may threaten competition. Mergers already notified before 1 July are unaffected.

title: CCPC welcomes draft EU merger guidelines but cautions against any relaxation of merger control
title_en: CCPC Endorses Draft EU Merger Guidelines with Emphasis on Protecting Competition and SME Viability
summary_en: Ireland's CCPC, along with six other medium-sized EU Member State competition authorities, welcomed new draft EU merger guidelines aimed at modernizing merger assessment with a unified framework. The guidelines recognize innovation, resilience, and sustainability as factors in competitive analysis. The authorities cautioned against excessive relaxation of merger control, emphasizing that protecting well-functioning competitive markets and SME interests should not be sacrificed for enabling large 'European champions.'

title: Infovoldik - ebaausad kaubandustavad
title_en: Unfair Trading Practices Guidance in Agricultural and Food Supply Chain
summary_en: The Estonian Competition Authority issued guidance on the Law on Prevention of Unfair Trading Practices in Agricultural and Food Supply Chain (PTEKS). The material explains which buyer actions are prohibited, when prior agreement between parties is required, and provides resources on compliance obligations, supervision mechanisms, and assistance options. The guidance applies to all businesses that buy or sell agricultural products and food in the supply chain.

title: Odluka o izravnim mjerama kontrole cijena određenih proizvoda i određenih kategorija proizvoda u trgovini na malo i Odluka o objavi cjenika i isticanju dodatne cijene kao mjera izravne kontrole cijene u trgovini na malo Izvješće o obavljenim inspekcijskim nadzorima od 7. veljače 2025. do 10. srpnja 2026. godine 14.07.2026.
title_en: Croatian State Inspectorate Report: Price Control Compliance Enforcement (February 2025 – July 2026)
summary_en:
The Croatian State Inspectorate released an enforcement report on price control measures for selected product categories in retail trade covering February 2025 through July 2026. Inspections identified 2,354 products displayed without proper visual identification labels, 438 products priced above maximum allowed ceilings, and widespread non-compliance with requirements to stock and display price-controlled items at mandated maximum prices across retail locations.

The scale of enforcement indicates active regulatory monitoring of retail compliance with price ceiling directives across product categories. For beverage suppliers whose products may fall under price-controlled categories, this signals heightened compliance risk and the need for supply chain partners and retail customers to maintain strict adherence to labeling, pricing disclosure, and point-of-sale pricing requirements.

title: Vorsicht bei Urlaubssouvenirs: Nicht alles darf mitreisen 14. Juli 2026 | Lesezeit 3 min Mit der Urlaubssaison rücken auch die Vorschriften zur Einfuhr von Pflanzen und Pflanzenerzeugnissen aus Nicht-EU-Ländern verstärkt in den Fokus. Am Flughafen Wien/Schwechat werden jährlich über 32 Millionen Reisende abgefertigt und vom Zoll mehr als…
title_en: EU plant health import regulations: phytosanitary certificate enforcement at EU borders
summary_en: EU regulations mandate phytosanitary certificates for nearly all plants and plant products imported from non-EU countries. Austrian customs confiscated approximately 3 tonnes of fruits, vegetables, cut flowers, and live plants lacking required plant health documentation between January and July 2026. Certain plant species and plant parts are prohibited from EU importation. Official plant health inspections are conducted at all EU entry points including Vienna Airport.
entities: AGES (Austrian Agency for Health and Food Security)

title: Informationen zu Prüfung, Bewertung & Zulassung
title_en: Pesticide Authorization and Approval under EU Regulation 1107/2009
summary_en: AGES conducts testing, evaluation, and authorization of plant protection products under EU Regulation 1107/2009 and Austrian Plant Protection Act 2011. Austria applies integrated pest management strategy emphasizing preventive and sustainable pesticide application. All plant protection products sold in EU markets must pass regulatory authorization. Regulation covers chemical and biological pesticides, herbicides, and fungicides used in agriculture including coffee cultivation.

title: EU-Düngeprodukte und Konformitätsbewertung
title_en: EU Fertilizer Products Regulation 2019/1009 and CE Marking Requirements
summary_en: EU Regulation 2019/1009 established harmonized requirements for fertilizer products marketed in the EU with CE marking. AGES is accredited (ISO 17065) for conformity assessment of fertilizer products. Regulation covers organic fertilizers, soil amendments, culture substrates, and biostimulants. Manufacturers, importers, and distributors regardless of origin must follow conformity procedures.

title: Nationale Referenzzentralen & Labors
title_en: National Reference Centers and Laboratories for Food Safety Monitoring
summary_en: Austria's AGES operates national reference laboratories for food safety across EU Member States, including specialized facilities for pesticide residues, mycotoxins, plant toxins, foodborne viruses, GMOs, and materials in food contact. These labs establish harmonized examination standards for official food and feed controls. Scope includes residues of veterinary drugs, animal feed additives, pesticides, and contaminants—directly applicable to imported agricultural products including coffee and tea.

title: Informationen zu Pflanzengesundheit
title_en: Plant Health Protection and Quarantine Pest Regulation
summary_en: AGES operates Austria's official plant protection service protecting agricultural production from quarantine pests (insects, mites, viruses, bacteria, fungi, invasive plants). Legal basis: Plant Protection Act 2018. Early detection and diagnosis of plant diseases are critical; quarantine pest occurrence must be reported to regional authorities. Protections target securing healthy plant production and food supply security.

title: Rückstände & Kontaminanten von A bis Z
title_en: AGES Residues and Contaminants Database for Food Safety Compliance
summary_en: AGES maintains a comprehensive reference database cataloging residues and contaminants in food products, including pesticides, heavy metals (lead, cadmium, mercury), mycotoxins (aflatoxins, furans), chemical byproducts (MCPDs, dioxins), and processing-generated substances (acrylamid). The database defines maximum residue levels (MRLs) and establishes compliance thresholds required for all food products sold in Austria and the EU.

title: Die Österreichische Ernährungspyramide
title_en: Updated Austrian Food-Based Dietary Guidelines: Plant-Based Emphasis & Climate Integration
summary_en: Austria has updated its official nutrition recommendations (FBDGs) to integrate environmental and climate factors alongside nutritional content, approved by the National Nutrition Commission. The new guidelines emphasize plant-based foods with minimal animal products, designed to reduce diet-related greenhouse gas emissions and land use by over 50% compared to current consumption patterns while meeting nutritional requirements.
entities: Competence Centre for Climate and Health

title: Lebensmittelkontrolle in Österreich
title_en: Austria's Official Food Control System: Farm-to-Consumer Oversight
summary_en: Austria operates a comprehensive official food control system enforcing EU-harmonized safety and labeling standards from production through retail. The system monitors for harmful microorganisms, pesticide residues, heavy metals, and chemical contaminants; coordinates inspections and sampling across federal, regional, and district authorities; and implements targeted surveillance programs to manage risks throughout the entire food chain.
entities: Austrian Federal Ministry of Social Affairs Health Care Consumer Protection
Austrian Federal Ministry of Agriculture Regions Forestry

title: Mehrjähriger Nationaler Kontrollplan
title_en: Austria's Multi-Year National Food Control Plan (MNKP)
summary_en: Austria's multi-year national control plan (MNKP) establishes a comprehensive official food safety framework to protect public health and ensure fair competition. The coordinated system covers food, animal feed, plant health, pesticide use, organic certification, and quality standards, with regular monitoring programs implemented across federal and regional authorities.
entities: Austrian Federal Ministry of Social Affairs Health Care Consumer Protection

title: One Health - Gesundheit für Mensch, Tier, Pflanzen und Umwelt
title_en: One Health Approach: Integrated Food System Sustainability in Austria
summary_en: Austria's AGES agency applies an integrated One Health approach across food systems, combining surveillance of antimicrobial resistance, food system resilience, and environmental impacts. The strategy emphasizes sustainable, circular-economy-based food production and evidence-based nutrition policy to reduce health risks and environmental footprint across the entire value chain.

title: Pflanzenschutzmittel Sachkundenachweis
title_en: Plant Protection Products (Pesticides) Expertise Certification Requirements in Austria
summary_en: Austria requires specialized training and certification (Sachkundenachweis) for staff selling plant protection products (pesticides), enforced under the Pflanzenschutzmittelverordnung 2011. The certification program includes initial training and mandatory recertification every six years, demonstrating Austria's stringent standards for pesticide stewardship that affect residue limits and supply chain practices.
entities: Austrian Federal Office for Food Safety (BAES)

title: EU Data Act A brief guide to the new legislation that aims to improve data sharing, governance, and innovation in the European Union.
title_en: EU Data Act: Mandatory Data Access Rights for Connected Consumer Products
summary_en: The EU Data Act establishes binding rules for data sharing and access from connected products across the EU. Users must be granted free and easy access to data generated by connected devices, or can request data-sharing with third parties; data holders may request compensation under fair, reasonable, and non-discriminatory (FRND) terms. For JDE Peet's, this legislation directly impacts smart coffee appliances (Tassimo, Senseo) that collect user data, requiring product redesign to enable mandatory user data access and establishing FRND compensation frameworks for third-party data requests.

title: A New ERA: The UK Employment Rights Act 2025 Explained Podcast The Employment Rights Act 2025 introduces far-reaching reforms that will reshape how organisations recruit, manage and retain their workforce.
title_en: UK Employment Rights Act 2025: Comprehensive Employment Law Reforms Effective
summary_en: The UK Employment Rights Act 2025 introduces far-reaching reforms affecting all UK employers, including enhanced day-one worker rights, reform of unfair dismissal qualifying periods, trade union reforms, and strengthened enforcement mechanisms. JDE Peet's UK operations must adapt recruitment, management, and retention practices to comply with these fundamental changes, representing one of the most significant transformations of UK employment regulation in a generation.

title: Commission adopts amendments to the Union Customs Code Implementing Act to streamline the application of non‑preferential rules of origin
title_en: Union Customs Code Amendment: Rules of Origin Simplification and ELAN System for US and Agricultural Goods
summary_en: The European Commission amended the Union Customs Code to enable electronic certificates of origin via the ELAN system for agricultural goods (mandatory October 2028) and introduced a new requirement for US-origin goods to provide proof of direct transport or non-alteration to qualify for adjusted customs duties and tariff quotas under Regulation (EU) 2026/1455. This strengthens enforcement against tariff circumvention.

title: European Commission proposes landmark tax simplification package to streamline compliance and boost competitiveness
title_en: EU Tax Simplification Package: Withholding Tax Abolition and R&D Investment Incentives (24 June 2026)
summary_en: The European Commission adopted a tax simplification package eliminating withholding taxes on cross-border dividends, interest, and royalties between EU companies, and establishing a common minimum R&D investment standard with full and immediate expensing of tangible assets. The package, estimated to save businesses €7.9 billion in compliance costs, extends parent-subsidiary exemptions to pension institutions and modernizes direct tax rules.

title: Commissioner Maroš Sefčovič hosts Implementation Dialogue on EU Customs Reform
title_en: EU Customs Reform Implementation Dialogue
summary_en: The European Commission held an Implementation Dialogue on 8 June 2026 to discuss practical aspects of the EU Customs Reform with industry and trade associations. The discussion focused on two main pillars: the EU Customs Data Hub (backbone of the reform but requiring robust cybersecurity and contingency measures) and the Trust and Check regime (offering faster clearances for compliant traders but raising concerns about smaller businesses' access). Stakeholders emphasized the need for clearer guidance, realistic timelines, detailed technical specifications, and harmonized application across Member States to ensure a smooth transition.

title: Commission welcomes Council agreement on strengthening CBAM
title_en: Council Agreement on Strengthening CBAM
summary_en: The EU Council agreed on 12 June 2026 to strengthen the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) following a December 2025 Commission proposal. The strengthening extends CBAM scope to certain downstream products, reinforces anti-circumvention measures, and adapts electricity rules. The mechanism is designed to tackle carbon leakage, support EU competitiveness, and accelerate decarbonization investment. Further discussions will occur in trialogue following the European Parliament's formal position adoption expected in September 2026.

title: CBAM webinar follow up: “How to make your decarbonisation efforts pay off”
title_en: CBAM Methodology Webinar: Emissions Monitoring and Compliance
summary_en: The EU Commission's DG TAXUD conducted a webinar to assist third-country producers in understanding CBAM methodology for the definitive period (starting 1 January 2026). Key topics included functional unit calculations, multifunctional production processes, embedded emissions reporting for precursors, and new system boundaries for iron, steel, and aluminium. Producers were advised to establish monitoring plans and verification obligations immediately, as 2026 is the first measurement year for actual emissions tracking and annual CBAM declarations.

title: Guidance and legal text on temporary flat fee on low-value imports which will apply until 1 July 2028
title_en: New €3 Flat Customs Duty on Low-Value Imports (July 2026–2028)
summary_en: Effective 1 July 2026, the EU is introducing a temporary €3 flat customs duty per item on low-value consignments (up to €150) imported from outside the EU, abolishing the previous duty-free de minimis exemption. The measure, part of the Customs Reform, aims to ensure fair competition and compliance with EU safety standards, as targeted inspections in 2025 found over 60% non-compliance in cosmetics, PPE, food supplements, toys, and electronics. The temporary duty applies until 1 July 2028, after which normal customs duties will apply depending on product type.

title: EU Customs Union: facts and figures
title_en: EU Customs Union: Facts and Figures 2025
summary_en: The EU customs union processed 630.9 million import and 580 million export items in 2025, collecting €30.7 billion in customs duties. The system handles 212 items declared per second, of which 173 are e-commerce, across 2,200 customs offices. Customs balances trade facilitation with protection of EU citizens and businesses.

title: EU and Türkiye: a new agreement to strengthen customs cooperation
title_en: EU and Türkiye: Customs Cooperation Agreement on Trusted Trader Recognition
summary_en: The EU and Türkiye strengthened their 1995 Customs Union with a new agreement mutually recognizing Authorised Economic Operator (AEO) trusted trader status. Customs authorities can now factor trusted trader status into risk assessments and control determinations, enhancing supply chain security and facilitating legitimate trade pending structured information exchange setup.

title: 9th Seminar of European Customs Chemists
title_en: 9th Seminar of European Customs Chemists: CBAM, Tariff Classification AI, Excise and Food Fraud
summary_en: DG TAXUD's Customs Laboratories European Network convened 190+ participants from 39 countries to discuss AI for tariff classification and data analysis, the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), food and medicine fraud detection, excise product compliance, and e-commerce customs challenges. Topics included non-tariff barriers, chemical analysis for classification, and international enforcement cooperation.

title: Sustainability, due diligence, responsible business
title_en: Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) and Responsible Business Practices
summary_en: The EU initiative on corporate sustainability due diligence and responsible business promotes sustainable corporate behaviour through mandatory due diligence requirements across supply chains, encompassing human rights, environmental standards, and accountability. The directive supports a just transition toward a sustainable economy and is part of the broader corporate governance reform agenda.

title: Guidelines to help companies disclose environmental and social information
title_en: Guidelines on Non-Financial and Climate-Related Reporting for Large Companies
summary_en: The Commission published non-binding guidelines to help large public interest entities (more than 500 employees) disclose environmental, social, and climate-related information consistently and comparably. Guidelines integrate TCFD recommendations and EU Taxonomy classifications, with dedicated supplements for climate-specific disclosures to support investor decision-making.

title: Delegated and implementing acts to the CSRD
title_en: CSRD Delegated Regulations (July 2026): Simplification and Voluntary Standards
summary_en: The Commission adopted delegated regulations on 3 July 2026 amending CSRD sustainability reporting standards to simplify certain requirements and establishing voluntary sustainability reporting standards for undertakings protected by the value chain cap. These measures reduce compliance burden while maintaining transparency requirements for investors and stakeholders.

title: Publication of the frequently asked questions on the implementation of the EU corporate sustainability reporting rules
title_en: FAQs published on Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) implementation
summary_en: European Commission published comprehensive frequently asked questions on 7 August 2024 clarifying CSRD implementation issues including scope, application dates, exemptions, and allowed use of estimates. FAQs clarify that companies may use estimates rather than mandatorily collecting value chain information from suppliers and partners where data is unavailable, reducing compliance administrative burden.

title: guidance on interoperability of European and global sustainability reporting standards
title_en: Guidance on interoperability between European (ESRS) and international (ISSB) sustainability standards
summary_en: EFRAG and the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) published guidance on 2 May 2024 demonstrating interoperability between European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) and global ISSB standards. The guidance confirms that EU companies reporting under ESRS can comply with ISSB standards with minimal additional effort, particularly for climate-related disclosures, supporting international comparability and reducing dual-reporting burden.

title: 2024 Commission work programme: Proposal for a Decision as regards the time limits for the adoption of sustainability reporting standards for certain sectors and for certain third-country undertakings
title_en: 2024 Commission Work Programme: Postponement of CSRD Reporting Deadlines
summary_en: The Commission's 2024 work programme includes proposals to reduce reporting requirements by 25%, including postponement of deadlines for sector-specific sustainability reporting standards under the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). These simplifications also affect the scope of the Accounting Directive and Benchmark Regulation, providing relief for reporting entities.

title: Adoption of the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS)
title_en: Adoption of European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS)
summary_en: The Commission adopted mandatory European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) for all companies subject to the CSRD, covering environmental, social, and governance issues including climate change, biodiversity, and human rights. The standards align with international initiatives (ISSB, GRI) to minimize double reporting and are being phased in by company category.

title: Proposal for a Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)
title_en: Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and EU Taxonomy Climate Delegated Act
summary_en: The Commission's sustainable finance package (April 2021) introduced the CSRD, extending mandatory sustainability reporting to all large companies and regulated market-listed undertakings (except listed micro-enterprises). Requirements include independent assurance, detailed reporting standards, digital tagging, and alignment with the EU Taxonomy Climate Delegated Act (effective 1 January 2022).

title: EFRAG reports on development of EU sustainability reporting standards
title_en: EFRAG Reports on Development of EU Sustainability Reporting Standards
summary_en: The European Financial Reporting Advisory Group (EFRAG) published reports proposing a comprehensive roadmap for EU sustainability reporting standards development and governance reforms. The standards are designed to support the European Green Deal ambitions and ensure consistency with the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation, Taxonomy Regulation, and forthcoming corporate governance requirements.

title: Public consultation on the review of the non-financial reporting directive
title_en: Public Consultation on Review of Non-Financial Reporting Directive
summary_en: The Commission conducted public consultation on the review of the Non-Financial Reporting Directive (2014/95/EU), which required large public interest entities (more than 500 employees) to disclose environmental and social information. This consultation process contributed to the development of the more comprehensive CSRD framework.

title: Targeted consultation on the guidelines on reporting climate-related information
title_en: Targeted Consultation on Climate-Related Financial Reporting Guidelines (2019)
summary_en: The Commission conducted a targeted consultation (February–March 2019) seeking stakeholder input on climate-related financial disclosure guidelines, receiving 114 responses from preparers and users of non-financial information. The consultation incorporated recommendations from the Financial Stability Board's Task Force on Climate-Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and informed the revision of non-financial reporting guidelines.
entities: Technical Expert Group on Sustainable Finance (TEG)

title: Recommendation on a voluntary sustainability reporting standard for small and medium-sized undertakings (VSME)
title_en: Voluntary Sustainability Reporting Standard (VSME) for small and medium enterprises
summary_en: European Commission adopted a recommendation on 30 July 2025 establishing a voluntary sustainability reporting standard for SMEs (VSME), developed by EFRAG. The standard reduces administrative burden on SMEs in JDE Peet's value chain by providing a harmonized template for responding to sustainability information requests from large companies subject to CSRD and CSDDD mandatory reporting obligations.

title: Adoption of a “quick‑fix” delegated act to revise the first set of ESRS
title_en: Quick-fix delegated act easing CSRD burden for wave one reporters (2024 baseline)
summary_en: European Commission adopted targeted amendments to the first set of European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) on 11 July 2025, reducing reporting burden for 'wave one' companies (those reporting on FY2024). The amendments allow these companies to continue omitting anticipated financial effects of sustainability-related risks in 2025–2026 filings, and extend phase-in provisions for companies with more than 750 employees, aligning them with smaller-company relief periods.

title: Legislative package to simplify EU rules and boost competitiveness
title_en: Omnibus I legislative package: regulatory simplification for CSRD, CSDDD, and sustainable finance
summary_en: European Commission adopted the Omnibus I legislative package on 26 February 2025, proposing far-reaching simplifications to sustainable finance reporting, CSRD, corporate sustainability due diligence (CSDDD), EU Taxonomy, and carbon border adjustment mechanisms. The package postpones CSRD application and CSDDD transposition timelines while reducing complexity for businesses, particularly SMEs and mid-caps, and refocusing requirements on largest companies with greater environmental/climate impact.

title: EU taxonomy for sustainable activities
title_en: EU Taxonomy for Sustainable Activities – classification system for corporate reporting
summary_en: The EU Taxonomy is a mandatory classification system for large companies to define and report economic activities aligned with net-zero 2050 and environmental objectives; it entered force July 2020. JDE Peet's, subject to the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), must classify coffee/tea production, distribution, and packaging activities against taxonomy criteria and disclose alignment in sustainability reports. Non-compliance or misclassification affects investor relations, capital access, and regulatory standing.

title: The European green bond standard – Supporting the transition
title_en: European Green Bond Standard – voluntary framework for sustainable financing
summary_en: The European Green Bond Standard, published November 2023, establishes criteria for green bonds financing sustainable projects using EU Taxonomy definitions, with supervision by ESMA. Implementation guidance and FAQs were published through November 2025. This framework enables large companies (including KDP/JDE Peet's) to issue green bonds for sustainable coffee/tea sourcing, production facilities, renewable energy, or circular packaging initiatives, potentially reducing borrowing costs for sustainability-linked projects.

title: EU labels for benchmarks (climate, ESG) and benchmarks’ ESG disclosures
title_en: EU climate and ESG benchmark standards – investor-facing sustainability metrics
summary_en: EU climate and ESG benchmark standards (in force since April 2020) establish minimum methodologies for climate transition and Paris-aligned benchmarks, with mandatory sustainability-related disclosure requirements for benchmark operators. These benchmarks are used by investors, asset managers, and financial institutions to assess and compare corporate climate and environmental performance, directly affecting how JDE Peet's and KDP are evaluated for investment decisions and capital allocation.

title: Insurance of climate-related disasters
title_en: Insurance of climate-related disasters – EU Climate Resilience Dialogue final report
summary_en: The EU Climate Resilience Dialogue concluded in July 2024, publishing a final report on addressing the 'climate protection gap'—insurance and financial losses from climate disasters. The report proposes risk reduction, risk-sharing through public-private partnerships, and insurance-based solutions, with detailed analysis of floods, wildfires, heatwaves, droughts, and storms. For agriculture-dependent supply chains like coffee/tea sourcing, this signals regulatory focus on climate resilience, potential changes to insurance availability, and expectations for business continuity planning.

title: Ten EU countries exceeding SUPD collection targets
title_en: Ten EU countries exceeding Single Use Plastics Directive collection targets
summary_en:
The European Commission's first report under the Single Use Plastics Directive (SUPD) shows that ten EU Member States (Estonia, Poland, Finland, Germany, Denmark, Sweden, Lithuania, Croatia, Slovakia, Belgium) have already met or exceeded 2025 collection targets for single-use plastic (SUP) beverage bottles, with six countries meeting 2030 targets of 90%.

The 2022 baseline shows 524,003 tonnes of SUP food containers and 152,037 tonnes of SUP beverage cups consumed in the EU, with 71% of beverage bottles separately collected. This directly impacts JDE Peet's beverage packaging compliance, requiring adherence to increasingly stringent collection and recovery targets through Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) schemes and promotion of sustainable alternatives across all European markets.
source_name: EU packaging waste / PPWR topic page

title: Facts about packaging and packaging waste
title_en: EU Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (PPWR): Core Requirements and Market Impact
summary_en: The Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (PPWR) establishes binding EU requirements: all packaging must be recyclable by 2030, mandatory increases in recycled-material content, reduction of unnecessary packaging, and promotion of reusable business models. Harmonized rules create a single EU market, potentially lowering business costs through standardized packaging across Member States and reducing transport costs for lighter materials. This directly impacts JDE Peet's packaged coffee and tea portfolio (Jacobs, L'OR, Senseo, Tassimo, Douwe Egberts, Kenco, Pickwick, Moccona).
source_name: EU packaging waste / PPWR topic page

title: Decision exempting certain economic operators that use pallet wrappings and straps from the 100% reuse requirements of these packaging formats
title_en: Commission Delegated Decision: Exemptions from Pallet Wrapping and Strap Reuse Requirements (PPWR)
summary_en: The European Commission issued a delegated decision (25 February 2026) exempting certain economic operators from the 100% reuse requirements for pallet wrappings and straps under PPWR. This regulatory decision provides targeted relief for specific operators in complying with stringent reuse mandates for logistics-related packaging materials and may offer compliance pathways for JDE Peet's supply chain.
source_name: EU packaging waste / PPWR topic page

title: Guidelines on the implementation of the Regulation for economic actors and Member States
title_en: Commission Guidance Document: PPWR Implementation Guidelines for Economic Actors and Member States
summary_en: The European Commission issued detailed implementation guidance (Notice C/2026/3702) for Regulation (EU) 2025/40 on packaging and packaging waste (PPWR). The document provides clarifications on compliance procedures, interpretation of requirements, and obligations for economic operators and national authorities. This guidance is essential for JDE Peet's to align packaging strategy with harmonized EU standards.
source_name: EU packaging waste / PPWR topic page

title: Workshop on implementing EC Roadmap on phasing out animal testing in pesticides and biocides
title_en: EC Roadmap Implementation Workshop: Phasing Out Animal Testing in Pesticide and Biocide Safety Assessment
summary_en: The European Commission published a roadmap (June 2026) to phase out animal testing for chemical safety assessments in pesticides and biocides. EFSA and ECHA are organizing a workshop to engage stakeholders in implementing this transition toward animal-free testing approaches. This regulatory development will impact pesticide approval standards and testing methodologies across the EU, potentially affecting agricultural sourcing and supply chain compliance for JDE Peet's coffee and tea operations.

title: Chemical contaminants in food and feed
title_en: EFSA Guidance on Chemical Contaminants in Food and Feed
summary_en: The European Food Safety Authority addresses chemical contaminants unintentionally present in food and feed, including natural toxins (mycotoxins, alkaloids), environmental contaminants (PCBs, dioxins, heavy metals, PFAS), and process contaminants (acrylamide, furan). EFSA's Panel on Contaminants conducts risk assessments that inform EU regulatory limits. Coffee and tea products are particularly susceptible to mycotoxins (aflatoxins) during agricultural sourcing and acrylamide during roasting, making contamination monitoring critical for supply chain compliance.

title: Food Labelling Information System (FLIS)
title_en: Food Labelling Information System (FLIS)
summary_en: The European Commission's Food Labelling Information System (FLIS) is an online tool that automatically retrieves mandatory EU labelling requirements in 23 languages for any food product and provides links to relevant legal provisions and guidance documents. FLIS serves as a reference documentation tool for ensuring compliance with EU labelling regulations (Regulation EU 1169/2011 and related directives), with access to authentic versions published in the Official Journal of the European Union.

title: Food information to consumers - legislation
title_en: EU Food Information to Consumers Regulation – Revision under Farm-to-Fork Strategy
summary_en: EU Regulation 1169/2011 on food information to consumers is being revised to introduce harmonised mandatory front-of-pack nutrition labelling, extend origin/provenance information requirements, and revise date marking rules. The revision will also mandate ingredient lists and nutrition declarations for all alcoholic beverages, directly impacting label compliance and packaging across JDE Peet's entire beverage portfolio in the EU.

title: May 2026 - Report on EU Agri-Fraud suspicions
title_en: EU Agri-Food Fraud Monitoring Report – May 2026
summary_en: The European Commission published its May 2026 monthly report on agri-food fraud suspicions coordinated through the Alert and Cooperation Network (ACN), which integrates the Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed (RASFF), Administrative Assistance and Cooperation network, and the Agri-Food Fraud Network. The report identifies cross-border fraud cases affecting coffee, tea, and other agricultural commodities in the food supply chain.

title: Monthly reports on EU Agri-Food Fraud suspicions
title_en: Monthly EU Agri-Food Fraud Suspicion Reports
summary_en: The European Commission publishes monthly reports on suspected fraud and deceptive practices in the agri-food chain, sourced from the Alert and Cooperation Network (ACN). These reports cover food, feed, food contact materials, and plant health items, enabling Member States and operators to identify emerging fraud patterns and supply chain vulnerabilities. The reports include cross-border non-compliances flagged through the Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed (RASFF), Administrative Assistance and Cooperation network (AAC), and Agri-Food Fraud Network (FFN).

title: Agri-Food fraud: What does it mean?
title_en: EU Agri-Food Fraud: Regulatory Definition and Operative Criteria
summary_en: EU agri-food fraud is defined as suspected intentional action by businesses or individuals to deceive purchasers and gain undue advantage in violation of Regulation (EU) 2017/625. Four operative criteria must all be met: violation of EU rules, customer deception (e.g., altered labelling or undeclared allergens), undue economic advantage, and verified intent (not accidental). Fraud can occur at any stage of production, processing, or trade and may hinder Single Market functioning and pose risks to human, animal, or plant health.

title: EU food waste: relevant legislation
title_en: EU Binding Food Waste Reduction Targets (2030)
summary_en: The Waste Framework Directive was amended in 2025 to establish binding food waste reduction targets for Member States by 2030: 10% reduction in processing and manufacturing, and 30% per-capita reduction at retail and consumption levels (restaurants, food services, households). The baseline is established from 2020 EU-wide monitoring data. Member States must evaluate and adapt waste prevention programmes, designate competent authorities for coordination, and implement prevention measures across the entire supply chain.

title: Frequently Asked Questions: Reducing Food Waste in the EU
title_en: EU Food Waste Impact and Reduction Targets (FAQ)
summary_en: The EU generates over 58 million tonnes of food waste annually (€132 billion loss), accounting for 16% of total greenhouse gas emissions from the food system. Households generate 53%, with the remainder from manufacturing (19%), food services (11%), retail (8%), and primary production (10%). The Commission proposes binding 10% reduction in processing/manufacturing and 30% per-capita reduction at retail and consumption by 2030, with formal progress review by end of 2027 and potential adjustment of targets thereafter.

title: International Day of Awareness of Food Loss and Waste - 29 September
title_en: International Day of Food Loss and Waste Awareness (29 September 2025)
summary_en: The UN-designated International Day of Awareness of Food Loss and Waste (29 September 2025) will feature an EC-FAO webinar on stakeholder engagement in food waste reduction. The webinar highlights the role of public-private partnerships, voluntary agreements, and multi-stakeholder platforms in advancing SDG Target 12.3 (halving per-capita food waste at retail/consumer level by 2030) and achieving EU food waste reduction targets. Participation by food businesses, policy makers, civil society, and academia is expected.

title: Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed (RASFF)
title_en: RASFF – Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed
summary_en: The EU operates RASFF as a 24/7 food safety alert system enabling rapid information exchange between member states and the European Commission to prevent public health risks across the food chain. RASFF notifications trigger immediate product recalls; the public RASFF Window database (searchable from 2020 onwards) provides transparency on notifications and enables rapid compliance verification by producers and distributors operating in EU markets.

title: EU action to address the energy crisis
title_en: EU state aid framework for energy crisis (AccelerateEU)
summary_en: The European Commission adopted AccelerateEU measures in April 2026 to mitigate Middle East-driven energy price spikes affecting EU economies. A temporary state aid framework targets energy-intensive industries, agriculture, fishery and transport through 31 December 2026, providing targeted relief to sectors most exposed to energy price volatility while coordinating strategic reserves and accelerating renewable energy transition.

title: Organic products: MEPs back measures to support EU production
title_en: Organic products: EU Parliament backs enhanced labeling and competition standards for protected EU production
summary_en: The EU Parliament's Agriculture Committee adopted measures to strengthen organic product regulations, requiring importers from third countries to meet equivalent EU standards and comply with additional production controls to use the EU organic logo. The legislation raises exemption thresholds for small operators (from €20,000 to €25,000 annual turnover and from 5,000 kg to 10,000 kg annual sales volume) and modifies livestock housing rules to reduce administrative costs, protecting EU organic producers from unfair external competition while maintaining sector reputation and consumer confidence.

title: Better enforcement of rules needed for a safer social media and online environment
title_en: Parliament calls for stronger enforcement of social media safety rules with restrictions on influencer marketing and algorithmic transparency
summary_en: The EU Parliament's Culture and Education Committee adopted measures to strengthen enforcement of online safety for minors, calling for algorithmic transparency, introduction of a "youth mode" that disables targeted advertising, and bans on addictive design features. The Parliament also urges development of an EU code of conduct for influencer marketing and protection against commercial exploitation of children through practices such as kidfluencing and sharenting.

title: LOFFEE - Partnership at eye level Cofresco Sustainability
title_en: Melitta Group Invests in Loffee Startup to Develop Lupin-Based Coffee Alternative
summary_en: Melitta Group acquired a strategic equity stake in Loffee, a Swiss startup specializing in lupin coffee production, positioning itself in the emerging alternative protein beverage market. The partnership leverages Melitta's industry expertise, distribution networks, and strategic contacts to support Loffee's product development and market expansion, enabling Melitta to capture growth in alternative coffee product categories.

title: Digital Convention @ Melitta - The Future of Marketing
title_en: Melitta Group Convenes Strategic Forum on AI-Driven Marketing and Digital Brand Management
summary_en: Melitta Group's operating divisions hosted a Digital Convention addressing artificial intelligence, data-driven campaigns, and digital brand transformation. Keynote speakers included OMR founder Philipp Westermeyer on digital-first growth, Dr. Wolff Group's digitization lead on offline-to-digital integration, and Trusted Shops COO Alastair Bruce on trust and AI challenges, highlighting competitive imperatives for consumer goods manufacturers adopting AI-enabled marketing strategies.

title: Melitta goes AI - Melina Innovation Work culture
title_en: Melitta Group Deploys Proprietary AI Assistant 'Melina' for Internal Operations and Data Security
summary_en: Melitta Group launched 'Melina,' a custom-developed artificial intelligence platform assisting German employees with copywriting, analytics, research, and brainstorming. The system operates within Melitta's proprietary cloud infrastructure to ensure data security and intellectual property protection, avoiding external AI services. This deployment reflects broader corporate strategy to adopt AI capabilities while maintaining control over sensitive business information.

title: Less waste, less artificial fertilizer - more income Regenerative Value Creation Coffee
title_en: Melitta Group Completes Regenerative Coffee Agriculture Project in Brazil: Reduced Chemical Fertilizer, Waste Valorization
summary_en: Melitta Group completed a three-year 'Back to the Roots' regenerative agriculture initiative in Santo Antônio do Âmparo, Brazil, training approximately 150 smallholder coffee farmers to reduce chemical fertilizer use and adopt circular economy waste-management practices. Participating farmers learned to produce compost from coffee cherry husks and organic farm waste; one farmer increased compost output from 5 to 15 tons and reduced synthetic fertilizer expenditure. A local cooperative was established to trade surplus compost at reasonable prices.

title: Recycle plastic waste - avoid marine and microplastics Sustainability Plastic
title_en: Melitta/Cofresco Fair Recycled Plastic Initiative Expands to India: Garbage-Bag Packaging from Recovered Plastics
summary_en: Melitta Group subsidiary Cofresco announced expansion of Fair Recycled Plastic, processing plastic waste collected in India for use in garbage bags sold under the Swirl® and handy bag® brands. The initiative addresses ocean plastic pollution (approximately 90% of oceanic plastic originates from ten Asian rivers) by collecting and recycling waste plastic into consumer packaging, positioning plastic recycling as the more environmentally friendly alternative to virgin material when consistently reused.

title: Melitta Innovation Trend Day 2024 Innovation Work culture
title_en: Melitta Group Innovation Trend Day 2024: Competitor Deployment of Generative AI (Melina 2.0) and Metaverse Strategy
summary_en: Melitta Group hosted a Trend Day innovation event in Dortmund with 100 employees exploring metaverse retail concepts, generative AI applications, and future consumer personas. The company showcased its proprietary GenAI solution 'Melina 2.0' for product concept development, customer service automation, and streamlined daily operations, demonstrating competitive investment in AI-driven customer experience and digital transformation aligned with 2030 technology trends.

title: REal MAdrid & Melitta Sports partnerships
title_en: Melitta Group Secures Real Madrid Official Coffee Partnership: Major Global Sports Marketing Move
summary_en: The Melitta Group announced in December 2025 that it became the official coffee partner of Real Madrid, succeeding eight years with Manchester United. Real Madrid commands 750+ million fans globally and 650+ million social media followers with strong presence in Iberian Peninsula, Asia, and South America. The partnership positions Melitta's coffee in premium VIP stadium experiences and digital channels alongside elite brand partners (Adidas, Emirates, Hewlett Packard, Nivea, Louis Vuitton), leveraging sports sponsorship to build global brand awareness and emotional fan loyalty.

title: Melitta's Innovation Center for the American Coffee World m-Lab@Melitta USA
title_en: Melitta Opens m-Lab Innovation Center in North America: R&D and Coffee Product Development Hub
summary_en: Melitta Group opened a 418-square-meter innovation center (m-Lab) in Cherry Hill, New Jersey focused on coffee product and business development. The facility includes R&D laboratory, professional coffee systems, pilot roasting (60kg Probat roaster), training programs, and serves as business incubator for new coffee concepts. The center hosted the National Coffee Association Next Gen Coffee Challenge, bringing young coffee professionals together for product development, demonstrating Melitta's commitment to innovation and talent development in the premium coffee segment.

title: 60 years of filter coffee machines 60 years of innovation
title_en: Melitta Commemorates 60 Years of Filter Coffee Machine Innovation in Consumer Market
summary_en: Melitta marks six decades of electric filter coffee machine production, beginning with market entry in 1965 as a pioneering innovation.
